techniques.technique_name: Search Open Technical Databases: Scan Databases
techniques.evidence_quote: browsing shodan (or well, technically zoomeye, chinese shodan), looking for exposed jenkins servers
an exposed jenkins server belonging to CommuteAir... we do have access to build workspaces
a file called application-prod.properties... hardcoded credentials and s3 bucket names... i have ended up finding AWS credentials
numerous s3 buckets, dozens of dynamodb tables, as well as various servers and much more
i now seemingly have access to pretty much their entire aws infrastructure via aws-cli
grabbing sample documents from various s3 buckets, going through flight plans and dumping some dynamodb tables
three csv files, employee_information.csv, NOFLY.CSV and SELECTEE.CSV. all commited to the repository in july 2022
the data is available for access (upon request) via DDoSecrets

attack_chain.technique_name: Search Open Technical Databases: Scan Databases
attack_chain.description: Attacker used ZoomEye (and Shodan) to search for publicly exposed Jenkins servers, identifying a CommuteAir Jenkins instance based on aviation-specific keywords like 'ACARS' and 'crew' in results.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: browsing shodan (or well, technically zoomeye, chinese shodan), looking for exposed jenkins servers that may contain some interesting goods

attack_chain.description: Attacker accessed the unauthenticated/misconfigured publicly exposed Jenkins server belonging to CommuteAir. The server allowed access to build workspaces for approximately 70 build jobs without requiring admin credentials.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: while we don't have anonymous admin access... we do have access to build workspaces. this means we get to see the repositories that were built for each one of the ~70 build jobs

attack_chain.description: Attacker browsed build workspace files and found hardcoded credentials in configuration files (e.g., application-prod.properties), including SFTP credentials for a Navtech/Navblue server, Navblue API credentials, and eventually AWS credentials with access to S3 buckets and other AWS infrastructure.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: a file called application-prod.properties... two minutes after finding said file im staring at filezilla connected to a navtech sftp server... hardcoded credentials there would allow me access to navblue apis... i have ended up finding AWS credentials

attack_chain.description: Using the exfiltrated AWS credentials and the AWS CLI, the attacker enumerated CommuteAir's entire AWS infrastructure, discovering numerous S3 buckets, dozens of DynamoDB tables, and various servers.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: i now seemingly have access to pretty much their entire aws infrastructure via aws-cli. numerous s3 buckets, dozens of dynamodb tables, as well as various servers and much more

attack_chain.description: Attacker examined the 'noflycomparison' and 'noflycomparisonv2' Jenkins build repository test data and discovered three committed CSV files: employee_information.csv, NOFLY.CSV, and SELECTEE.CSV — the last two constituting a copy of the 2019 TSA No Fly List with over 1.56 million entries.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: for the first time actually take a peek at the test data in the repository. and there it is. three csv files, employee_information.csv, NOFLY.CSV and SELECTEE.CSV. all commited to the repository in july 2022

attack_chain.description: Attacker accessed multiple AWS S3 buckets and DynamoDB tables, collecting PII for ~900 CommuteAir employees (full names, addresses, phone numbers, passport numbers, pilot license numbers), flight plans, trip sheets, booking image attachments, and airplane maintenance data.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: grabbing sample documents from various s3 buckets, going through flight plans and dumping some dynamodb tables. at this point i had found pretty much all PII imaginable for each of their crew members

initial_access_vector: Publicly exposed Jenkins CI/CD server discovered via ZoomEye/Shodan, accessible without admin authentication, exposing build workspaces for ~70 jobs
persistence_mechanism: None identified — attacker accessed systems directly via hardcoded credentials and AWS CLI; no persistence implants described
privilege_escalation: Lateral movement from Jenkins build workspace access to AWS infrastructure via hardcoded AWS credentials found in application configuration files (application-prod.properties)
